The best way to enjoy our comforts is, to give them up to God; he will then again give them as shall be the best for us. Let us look how far our faith has caused the like obedience, when we have been called to lesser acts of self-denial, or to make smaller sacrifices to our duty. Have we given up what was called for, fully believing that the Lord would make up all our losses, and even bless us by the most afflicting dispensations?
11.m:
We often need to leave behind our connections, interests, and comfort if we follow God’s call. If we believe like Abraham did, we’ll obey even if we don’t know what might happen next. We’ll stay faithful and trust that God will keep His promises.

Abraham showed his faith by following God’s command without question. Sarah believed God’s promise too. She trusted Him completely, knowing He would fulfill it.

Sometimes people who know about God’s promises wait a long time before seeing them come true. But faith helps us see these blessings even when they’re far away. It lets us love and be happy about things we haven’t gotten yet, just like travelers on a journey.

Faith helps believers face death bravely and say goodbye cheerfully to this world and its joys and troubles. Those who turn from sin to follow God never want to go back. True believers eagerly seek the heavenly reward. The stronger their faith, the more they desire it.

Even if they were lowly by birth, stained by sin, or poor in life, God isn’t embarrassed to call Himself the God of believers. They shouldn’t feel ashamed either, whether others respect or ignore them. Most importantly, they should avoid doing anything that shames God.

The biggest test of faith recorded is when Abraham offered Isaac. Each step showed how much he trusted God.

When we doubt or fear, we should think carefully about God’s power, like Abraham did. Giving up our comforts to God means He’ll return them—or better ones—when it’s right.

Let’s consider how well our faith leads us to obey when faced with small challenges or sacrifices for doing what’s right.

Did we give up what we were asked to, trusting that the Lord would replace everything we lost and even bless us through the hardest times?

11.o: Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau, concerning things to come. Things present are not the best things; no man knoweth love or hatred by having them or wanting them. Jacob lived by faith, and he died by faith, and in faith. Though the grace of faith is of use always through our whole lives, it is especially so when we come to die. Faith has a great work to do at last, to help the believer to die to the Lord, so as to honour him, by patience, hope, and joy. Joseph was tried by temptations to sin, by persecution for keeping his integrity; and he was tried by honours and power in the court of Pharaoh, yet his faith carried him through. It is a great mercy to be free from wicked laws and edicts; but when we are not so, we must use all lawful means for our security. In this faith of Moses' parents there was a mixture of unbelief, but God was pleased to overlook it. Faith gives strength against the sinful, slavish fear of men; it sets God before the soul, shows the vanity of the creature, and that all must give way to the will and power of God. The pleasures of sin are, and will be, but short; they must end either in speedy repentance or in speedy ruin. The pleasures of this world are for the most part the pleasures of sin; they are always so when we cannot enjoy them without deserting God and his people. Suffering is to be chosen rather than sin; there being more evil in the least sin, than there can be in the greatest suffering. God's people are, and always have been, a reproached people. Christ accounts himself reproached in their reproaches; and thus they become greater riches than the treasures of the richest empire in the world. Moses made his choice when ripe for judgment and enjoyment, able to know what he did, and why he did it. It is needful for persons to be seriously religious; to despise the world, when most capable of relishing and enjoying it. Believers may and ought to have respect to the recompence of reward. By faith we may be fully sure of God's providence, and of his gracious and powerful presence with us. Such a sight of God will enable believers to keep on to the end, whatever they may meet in the way. It is not owing to our own righteousness, or best performances, that we are saved from the wrath of God; but to the blood of Christ, and his imputed righteousness. True faith makes sin bitter to the soul, even while it receives the pardon and atonement. All our spiritual privileges on earth, should quicken us in our way to heaven. The Lord will make even Babylon fall before the faith of his people, and when he has some great thing to do for them, he raises up great and strong faith in them. A true believer is desirous, not only to be in covenant with God, but in communion with the people of God; and is willing to fare as they fare. By her works Rahab declared herself to be just. That she was not justified by her works appears plainly; because the work she did was faulty in the manner, and not perfectly good, therefore it could not be answerable to the perfect justice or righteousness of God.
11.m:
Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau about future events. What happens now isn’t always the best thing. We don't really know if we’ll love or hate something until we experience it.

Jacob lived by faith and died by faith too. Faith helps us throughout life, but it’s especially important when we’re dying. Faith helps believers die well, showing honor to God through patience, hope, and joy.

Joseph faced many tests. He resisted temptation and stood firm under persecution. He also handled power and honors well because of his faith. When bad laws were around, Joseph used all legal ways to stay safe.

Moses’ parents had some doubt mixed with their faith, but God overlooked it. Faith strengthens us against fearing people who might harm us. Sin’s pleasure is brief—it ends quickly in regret or ruin. Worldly pleasures often lead to sinning against God.

Suffering is better than sinning. Even small sins are worse than big sufferings. God’s people have always been mocked. Jesus sees their troubles as His own. This makes their rewards greater than anything an empire could offer.

When Moses chose to follow God, he knew what he was doing. He did it wisely after growing up and understanding everything clearly. People should take religion seriously even when they can enjoy worldly things. Believers look forward to rewards from God.

Faith lets us trust that God watches over us and helps us in every situation.

Seeing God helps believers stay faithful until the end, no matter what happens along the way. We aren’t saved because of our own goodness or best efforts, but because of Jesus’ blood and his righteousness given to us. Faith makes sin feel bad, even as it brings forgiveness and healing. Our spiritual blessings here on Earth should motivate us to reach heaven. The Lord will cause Babylon to fall through the faith of his people. When he needs to do big things for them, he gives them strong faith. A true believer wants more than just a relationship with God; they want to connect with other believers too. Rahab showed she was just by her actions, though her deeds weren’t perfect, so they couldn’t save her. She needed God’s perfect righteousness to be made right.

11.o: After all our searches into the Scriptures, there is more to be learned from them. We should be pleased to think, how great the number of believers was under the Old Testament, and how strong their faith, though the objects of it were not then so fully made known as now. And we should lament that now, in gospel times, when the rule of faith is more clear and perfect, the number of believers should be so small, and their faith so weak. It is the excellence of the grace of faith, that, while it helps men to do great things, like Gideon, it keeps from high and great thoughts of themselves. Faith, like Barak's, has recourse unto God in all dangers and difficulties, and then makes grateful returns to God for all mercies and deliverances. By faith, the servants of God shall overcome even the roaring lion that goeth about seeking whom he may devour. The believer's faith endures to the end, and, in dying, gives him victory over death and all his deadly enemies, like Samson. The grace of God often fixes upon very undeserving and ill-deserving persons, to do great things for them and by them. But the grace of faith, wherever it is, will put men upon acknowledging God in all their ways, as Jephthah. It will make men bold and courageous in a good cause. Few ever met with greater trials, few ever showed more lively faith, than David, and he has left a testimony as to the trials and acts of faith, in the book of Psalms, which has been, and ever will be, of great value to the people of God. Those are likely to grow up to be distinguished for faith, who begin betimes, like Samuel, to exercise it. And faith will enable a man to serve God and his generation, in whatever way he may be employed. The interests and powers of kings and kingdoms, are often opposed to God and his people; but God can easily subdue all that set themselves against him. It is a greater honour and happiness to work righteousness than to work miracles. By faith we have comfort of the promises; and by faith we are prepared to wait for the promises, and in due time to receive them. And though we do not hope to have our dead relatives or friends restored to life in this world, yet faith will support under the loss of them, and direct to the hope of a better resurrection. Shall we be most amazed at the wickedness of human nature, that it is capable of such awful cruelties to fellow-creatures, or at the excellence of Divine grace, that is able to bear up the faithful under such cruelties, and to carry them safely through all? What a difference between God's judgement of a saint, and man's judgment! The world is not worthy of those scorned, persecuted saints, whom their persecutors reckon unworthy to live. They are not worthy of their company, example, counsel, or other benefits. For they know not what a saint is, nor the worth of a saint, nor how to use him; they hate, and drive such away, as they do the offer of Christ and his grace. (Heb 11:39)
11.m:
After studying the Scriptures, there is still much to learn from them. We should be happy knowing how many believed during the Old Testament times and how strong their faith was, even if what they believed wasn't as clearly explained as it is today. Now, in the New Testament times, when the rules of faith are clearer, fewer people believe and those who do have weaker faith. 

Faith helps people do big things, like Gideon did, without thinking too highly of themselves. Like Barak, faith leads us to trust God in danger and thank Him after He saves us. With faith, God’s servants can defeat the evil one, just like Samson did. Even at the end of life, faith gives victory over death and other fears.

God often chooses people who don’t deserve it to do amazing things. Faith makes people acknowledge God in everything they do, like Jephthah did. It gives courage to stand firm in doing right, as David showed through his faith. His psalms tell us about testing faith and overcoming trials, which help others too. Starting early with faith, like Samuel, helps people become known for their belief later on.

Faith lets people serve God well, no matter what they do. Kings and nations sometimes try to stop God’s people, but God always wins. Doing good is better than performing miracles. Faith brings joy from God’s promises and helps us wait patiently for them.

Even if we don’t expect our loved ones who died to come back to life in this world, faith helps us cope with their loss and gives us hope for a better future.

Are we more surprised by how cruel people can be to each other, or by how much God’s love supports believers through that cruelty?

There’s a big difference between how the world judges good people and how God sees them. The world thinks these holy people aren’t even worth having around. But God knows they’re valuable and treats them well.

People who hate and push away good people are like those who reject Jesus and his kindness. (Heb 11:39)

11.o: The world considers that the righteous are not worthy to live in the world, and God declares the world is not worthy of them. Though the righteous and the worldlings widely differ in their judgment, they agree in this, it is not fit that good men should have their rest in this world. Therefore God receives them out of it. The apostle tells the Hebrews, that God had provided some better things for them, therefore they might be sure that he expected as good things from them. As our advantages, with the better things God has provided for us, are so much beyond theirs, so should our obedience of faith, patience of hope, and labour of love, be greater. And unless we get true faith as these believers had, they will rise up to condemn us at the last day. Let us then pray continually for the increase of our faith, that we may follow these bright examples, and be, with them, at length made perfect in holiness and happiness, and shine like the sun in the kingdom of our Father for evermore.
11.m:
The world thinks that good people don’t deserve to live here, and God agrees that the world isn’t good enough for them. Even though the righteous and those who aren’t see things differently, they both think good people shouldn’t stay in this world. So God takes the righteous away from it.

The apostle told the Hebrews that God had prepared better things for them, which means they could expect more from them too. Since God gave them such great gifts, they should show even more faith, patience, and love than others do.

If we don’t have real faith like these believers did, they will judge us on Judgment Day. We should keep asking God to help us grow in faith, so we can follow their example. Then one day, we’ll join them in being holy and happy forever, shining brightly in God’s kingdom.

12.o: The persevering obedience of faith in Christ, was the race set before the Hebrews, wherein they must either win the crown of glory, or have everlasting misery for their portion; and it is set before us. By the sin that does so easily beset us, understand that sin to which we are most prone, or to which we are most exposed, from habit, age, or circumstances. This is a most important exhortation; for while a man's darling sin, be it what it will, remains unsubdued, it will hinder him from running the Christian race, as it takes from him every motive for running, and gives power to every discouragement. When weary and faint in their minds, let them recollect that the holy Jesus suffered, to save them from eternal misery. By stedfastly looking to Jesus, their thoughts would strengthen holy affections, and keep under their carnal desires. Let us then frequently consider him. What are our little trials to his agonies, or even to our deserts? What are they to the sufferings of many others? There is a proneness in believers to grow weary, and to faint under trials and afflictions; this is from the imperfection of grace and the remains of corruption. Christians should not faint under their trials. Though their enemies and persecutors may be instruments to inflict sufferings, yet they are Divine chastisements; their heavenly Father has his hand in all, and his wise end to answer by all. They must not make light of afflictions, and be without feeling under them, for they are the hand and rod of God, and are his rebukes for sin. They must not despond and sink under trials, nor fret and repine, but bear up with faith and patience. God may let others alone in their sins, but he will correct sin in his own children. In this he acts as becomes a father. Our earthly parents sometimes may chasten us, to gratify their passion, rather than to reform our manners. But the Father of our souls never willingly grieves nor afflicts his children. It is always for our profit. Our whole life here is a state of childhood, and imperfect as to spiritual things; therefore we must submit to the discipline of such a state. When we come to a perfect state, we shall be fully reconciled to all God's chastisement of us now. God's correction is not condemnation; the chastening may be borne with patience, and greatly promote holiness. Let us then learn to consider the afflictions brought on us by the malice of men, as corrections sent by our wise and gracious Father, for our spiritual good.
12.m:
The faithful following of Christ was the goal set before the Hebrew people. They had to choose between winning the prize of heaven or facing endless suffering. The same challenge is set before us today.

By the "sin that does so easily beset us," we mean the sin we find hardest to resist, whether because of habits, age, or other reasons. This is a very serious warning. As long as someone clings to their favorite sin, they won't run the Christian race well. Sin takes away all motivation and makes everything seem too hard.

When believers get tired and discouraged, they should remember that Jesus suffered to save them from eternal pain. Looking at Jesus strengthens good feelings and controls selfish desires. We should often think about him.

How do our small troubles compare to Jesus' suffering or even to what we deserve? How do they compare to the suffering of others? Believers tend to get worn out and lose heart during trials. This comes from the weakness in our character and leftover sinful nature.

Christians shouldn’t give up when faced with difficulties. Even if bad people cause trouble, God uses those situations to teach and shape us. He corrects his children to help them grow better. 

We shouldn’t ignore our problems or become numb to them. These are God’s ways of correcting us for our mistakes. We need to stay strong and patient through trials. God might leave others alone in their sins, but he disciplines his children out of love. 

Sometimes human parents punish just to vent anger, but God never punishes out of anger. His corrections always aim to benefit us. Life on earth is like being a child learning to follow rules. We must accept the training until we reach full maturity. Then we’ll see that all God’s corrections were for our good.

God’s punishment is not meant to destroy us. We should endure it patiently because it helps us become more holy. So let’s see the troubles that come from other people’s meanness as lessons from our loving God to help us spiritually.

12.o: A burden of affliction is apt to make the Christian's hands hang down, and his knees grow feeble, to dispirit him and discourage him; but against this he must strive, that he may better run his spiritual race and course. Faith and patience enable believers to follow peace and holiness, as a man follows his calling constantly, diligently, and with pleasure. Peace with men, of all sects and parties, will be favourable to our pursuit of holiness. But peace and holiness go together; there can be not right peace without holiness. Where persons fail of having the true grace of God, corruption will prevail and break forth; beware lest any unmortified lust in the heart, which seems to be dead, should spring up, to trouble and disturb the whole body. Falling away from Christ is the fruit of preferring the delights of the flesh, to the blessing of God, and the heavenly inheritance, as Esau did. But sinners will not always have such mean thoughts of the Divine blessing and inheritance as they now have. It agrees with the profane man's disposition, to desire the blessing, yet to despise the means whereby the blessing is to be gained. But God will neither sever the means from the blessing, nor join the blessing with the satisfying of man's lusts. God's mercy and blessing were never sought carefully and not obtained.
12.m: Troubles can make a Christian feel weak and discouraged, but he must fight against those feelings so he can keep running his spiritual race well. Faith and patience help believers stay focused on living peacefully and holy lives, just like someone keeps working at their job happily and steadily. Being peaceful with everyone helps us pursue holiness too. Peace and holiness go hand in hand—there’s no real peace without holiness. When people lack God’s grace, their bad habits take over. Be careful that hidden sins don’t rise up and cause problems. People who leave Christ do so because they choose earthly pleasures over God’s blessings and heaven. Sinners often think little of God’s gifts and ways to get them. But God won't separate the way to His blessings from the blessings themselves, nor give blessings while allowing sinful desires. Those who truly seek God’s mercy and blessings usually find them.

12.o: Mount Sinai, on which the Jewish church state was formed, was a mount such as might be touched, though forbidden to be so, a place that could be felt; so the Mosaic dispensation was much in outward and earthly things. The gospel state is kind and condescending, suited to our weak frame. Under the gospel all may come with boldness to God's presence. But the most holy must despair, if judged by the holy law given from Sinai, without a Saviour. The gospel church is called Mount Zion; there believers have clearer views of heaven, and more heavenly tempers of soul. All the children of God are heirs, and every one has the privileges of the first-born. Let a soul be supposed to join that glorious assembly and church above, that is yet unacquainted with God, still carnally-minded, loving this present world and state of things, looking back to it with a lingering eye, full of pride and guile, filled with lusts; such a soul would seem to have mistaken its way, place, state, and company. It would be uneasy to itself and all about it. Christ is the Mediator of this new covenant, between God and man, to bring them together in this covenant; to keep them together; to plead with God for us, and to plead with us for God; and at length to bring God and his people together in heaven. This covenant is made firm by the blood of Christ sprinkled upon our consciences, as the blood of the sacrifice was sprinkled upon the altar and the victim. This blood of Christ speaks in behalf of sinners; it pleads not for vengeance, but for mercy. See then that you refuse not his gracious call and offered salvation. See that you do not refuse Him who speaketh from heaven, with infinite tenderness and love; for how can those escape, who turn from God in unbelief or apostacy, while he so graciously beseeches them to be reconciled, and to receive his everlasting favour! God's dealing with men under the gospel, in a way of grace, assures us, that he will deal with the despisers of the gospel, in a way of judgment. We cannot worship God acceptably, unless we worship him with reverence and godly fear. Only the grace of God enables us to worship God aright. God is the same just and righteous God under the gospel as under the law. The inheritance of believers is secured to them; and all things pertaining to salvation are freely given in answer to prayer. Let us seek for grace, that we may serve God with reverence and godly fear.
12.m:
Mount Sinai, where the Jewish church began, was a mountain that people could touch even though they were told not to. It was very physical and earthly. 

The gospel, however, is gentle and fits our weaknesses. In the gospel, everyone can go boldly before God. But anyone trying to follow the strict laws from Sinai without Jesus would fail.

The gospel church is called Mount Zion. Believers there see heaven clearly and have more spiritual thoughts. All God’s children are heirs with equal rights.

Imagine someone joining the church in heaven who doesn’t know God well, still loves the world, looks back longingly, and is proud and deceitful. That person would feel out of place and disturb others.

Jesus is the mediator of the new covenant, bringing God and humans together. He keeps them united, pleads with God for us, and with us for God. He brings us to heaven.

This covenant is sealed by Jesus’ blood, just as animal sacrifices were sprinkled on altars. His blood asks for mercy, not punishment. Don’t miss God’s offer of salvation.

Don’t reject the One speaking from heaven with great kindness and love. How can those who turn away from God avoid judgment?

God deals kindly with those who believe the gospel. But those who reject it face judgment. Worshiping God properly needs respect and fear. Only God’s grace helps us truly worship.

God is still fair and right now, just like he was back then. Believers will get their reward, and everything needed for salvation is freely given in response to prayers. We should ask for grace so we can serve God with respect and fear.

13.o: The design of Christ in giving himself for us, is, that he may purchase to himself a peculiar people, zealous of good works; and true religion is the strongest bond of friendship. Here are earnest exhortations to several Christian duties, especially contentment. The sin opposed to this grace and duty is covetousness, an over-eager desire for the wealth of this world, with envy of those who have more than ourselves. Having treasures in heaven, we may be content with mean things here. Those who cannot be so, would not be content though God raised their condition. Adam was in paradise, yet not contented; some angels in heaven were not contented; but the apostle Paul, though abased and empty, had learned in every state, in any state, to be content. Christians have reason to be contented with their present lot. This promise contains the sum and substance of all the promises; “I will never, no, never leave thee, no, never forsake thee.” In the original there are no less than five negatives put together, to confirm the promise: the true believer shall have the gracious presence of God with him, in life, at death, and for ever. Men can do nothing against God, and God can make all that men do against his people, to turn to their good.
13.m:
Christ gave himself for us to buy a special group of people who love doing good deeds. True religion makes friends stronger. 

There are strong calls to follow different Christian duties, especially being content. Covetousness, which means wanting too much money and envying others who have more, goes against this. We should be happy even if we don’t have much because we know we have treasure in heaven. People who aren't content wouldn't be satisfied even if God improved their situation. 

Adam wasn't content in paradise, and some angels weren't content in heaven. But the Apostle Paul, even when poor and humble, could find contentment in any situation. Christians should be happy with what they have now. 

This promise sums up all other promises: "I will never, ever leave you or abandon you." There are five negative words in the original to show how sure this promise is. A true believer will always have God’s help—through life, at death, and forever. 

People can't stop God from doing what He wants. Even when others harm His people, God turns it around to be good for them.

13.o: The instructions and examples of ministers, who honourably and comfortably closed their testimony, should be particularly remembered by survivors. And though their ministers were some dead, others dying, yet the great Head and High Priest of the church, the Bishop of their souls, ever lives, and is ever the same. Christ is the same in the Old Testament day. as in the gospel day, and will be so to his people for ever, equally merciful, powerful, and all-sufficient. Still he fills the hungry, encourages the trembling, and welcomes repenting sinners: still he rejects the proud and self-righteous, abhors mere profession, and teaches all whom he saves, to love righteousness, and to hate iniquity. Believers should seek to have their hearts established in simple dependence on free grace, by the Holy Spirit, which would comfort their hearts, and render them proof against delusion. Christ is both our Altar and our Sacrifice; he sanctifies the gift. The Lord's supper is the feast of the gospel passover. Having showed that keeping to the Levitical law would, according to its own rules, keep men from the Christian altar, the apostle adds, Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp; go forth from the ceremonial law, from sin, from the world, and from ourselves. Living by faith in Christ, set apart to God through his blood, let us willingly separate from this evil world. Sin, sinners, nor death, will not suffer us to continue long here; therefore let us go forth now by faith and seek in Christ the rest and peace which this world cannot afford us. Let us bring our sacrifices to this altar, and to this our High Priest, and offer them up by him. The sacrifice of praise to God, we should offer always. In this are worship and prayer, as well as thanksgiving.
13.m:
Ministers who finished their work honorably should be remembered by those who survived them. Even if some ministers died or were close to death, Jesus, the head of the church, continues to live forever and remains the same. He was the same in the Old Testament times as he is today, and he will always be merciful, powerful, and enough for his people. He feeds the hungry, comforts the scared, and welcomes those who turn away from sin. But he rejects the proud and self-righteous, hates empty talk, and teaches everyone he saves to love what’s right and hate wrong things.

Believers should trust completely in God’s kindness, given by the Holy Spirit. This trust brings comfort and protects them from being misled. Jesus is both our altar and our sacrifice—he makes the offering holy. The Lord’s Supper is like a meal celebrating the Passover in the New Testament.

After showing that following the old laws wouldn’t allow people to come to the new Christian altar, the apostle said, “Let’s go out to Jesus.” We need to leave behind the old ceremonies, sins, the world, and even ourselves. By living by faith in Christ, separated to God through his blood, we should willingly stay away from this sinful world. Sin, sinners, and death won’t let us stay here long, so we should go to Christ now for the peace and rest this world can’t give us. Bring your offerings to this altar and to this high priest, and present them through him. Always offer the sacrifice of praise to God, including worship, prayers, and thanks.

13.o: We must, according to our power, give to the necessities of the souls and bodies of men: God will accept these offerings with pleasure, and will accept and bless the offerers through Christ. The apostle then states what is their duty to living ministers; to obey and submit to them, so far as is agreeable to the mind and will of God, made known in his word. Christians must not think themselves too wise, too good, or too great, to learn. The people must search the Scriptures, and so far as the ministers teach according to that rule, they ought to receive their instructions as the word of God, which works in those that believe. It is the interest of hearers, that the account their ministers give of them may be with joy, and not with grief. Faithful ministers deliver their own souls, but the ruin of a fruitless and faithless people will be upon their own heads. The more earnestly the people pray for their ministers, the more benefit they may expect from their ministry. A good conscience has respect to all God's commands, and all our duty. Those who have this good conscience, yet need the prayers of others. When ministers come to a people who pray for them, they come with greater satisfaction to themselves, and success to the people. We should seek all our mercies by prayer. God is the God of peace, fully reconciled to believers; who has made a way for peace and reconciliation between himself and sinners, and who loves peace on earth, especially in his churches. He is the Author of spiritual peace in the hearts and consciences of his people. How firm a covenant is that which has its foundation in the blood of the Son of God! The perfecting of the saints in every good work, is the great thing desired by them, and for them; and that they may at length be fitted for the employment and happiness of heaven. There is no good thing wrought in us, but it is the work of God. And no good thing is wrought in us by God, but through Christ, for his sake and by his Spirit.
13.m:
We should help people with both their physical needs and their spiritual ones. God accepts these gifts happily and blesses those who give through Jesus. 

The apostle explains what we owe to living ministers. We should listen to them and follow their guidance, as long as it matches what God’s Word teaches. Christians shouldn’t think they know everything. They need to study the Bible too.

People should look up the Bible first. Then if the minister teaches things that match the Bible, they should take those teachings seriously, seeing them as God’s words. This helps the minister feel happy about their work, not sad.

Good ministers protect their own souls. But unfaithful people might bring trouble on themselves. When people pray for their ministers, they get more out of their sermons.

Having a clear conscience means following all of God’s rules. Even people with a good conscience still need others to pray for them. Ministers feel better when people pray for them, and the congregation benefits more.

We should ask God for all our blessings through prayer. God is the God of peace, fully forgiving believers. He made a way for peace between Himself and sinners. He wants peace in His churches.

God brings inner peace to believers’ hearts and minds. The agreement based on Jesus' blood is very strong. Helping believers grow spiritually is important. We want them ready for heaven.

Nothing good happens within us unless God makes it happen. And nothing good comes from God without Jesus, for His sake and by His Spirit.

13.o: So bad are men, and even believers, through the remainders of their corruption, that when the most important, comfortable doctrine is delivered to them for their own good, and that with the most convincing evidence, there is need of earnest entreaty and exhortation that they would bear it, and not fall out with it, neglect it, or reject it. It is good to have the law of holy love and kindness written in the hearts of Christians, one towards another. Religion teaches men true civility and good breeding. It is not ill-tempered or uncourteous. Let the favour of God be toward you, and his grace continually working in you, and with you, bringing forth the fruits of holiness, as the first-fruits of glory.
13.m:
People, including believers, still have some sin left in them. Even when they hear very helpful truths meant to benefit them, presented with strong proof, they might ignore it or refuse it unless someone urges them strongly to pay attention.

It’s good for Christian friends to show love and kindness to each other. Religion helps people learn real politeness and good manners. 

Being religious doesn’t mean being rude. Instead, let God’s favor be on you all the time. May His grace work within you and help you produce goodness, which will lead to greater rewards in heaven.
